steps.title: Track the survival chain
steps.content: The passage says many vulnerable native forest plants on Oahu depend on birds eating their fruits and spreading seeds to new locations. It also says the native fruit-eating birds are extinct, while introduced fruit-eating birds are now common and have been confirmed to spread plant seeds. The inference should connect these non-native birds to continued plant survival.

steps.title: Stay within the evidence
steps.content: Choice A compares current seed dispersal with the extinct native birds, but the passage gives no numbers for that historical comparison. Choice B invents a preference for native fruits over non-native fruits. Choice D speculates about other activities beyond seed spreading. Since vulnerable plants need seed dispersal and non-native birds now provide it, those birds may be necessary for plants such as the olona shrub to survive. The answer is C.
